title: William Damasus Lindanus

infobox.value: or **Van der Lindt** (1525 – 2 November 1588) was a 16th-century [Bishop of Roermond](/wiki/Bishop_of_Roermond "Bishop of Roermond") and [Bishop of Ghent](/wiki/Bishop_of_Ghent "Bishop of Ghent").

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e5/Portret_van_Willem_van_der_Linden_Gvilielmvs_Lindanvs_Episcop_Gandensis_%28titel_op_object%29_Portretten_van_beroemde_Nederlandse_en_Vlaamse_geleerden_%28serietitel%29_Illustrium_Galliae_Belgicae_scriptorum_icones_et_elogi_%28s%2C_RP-P-1906-676.jpg/250px-thumbnail.jpg)](/wiki/File:Portret_van_Willem_van_der_Linden_Gvilielmvs_Lindanvs_Episcop_Gandensis_(titel_op_object)_Portretten_van_beroemde_Nederlandse_en_Vlaamse_geleerden_(serietitel)_Illustrium_Galliae_Belgicae_scriptorum_icones_et_elogi_(s,_RP-P-1906-676.jpg)

W.D. Lindanus (1588)

**William Damasus Lindanus** or **Van der Lindt** (1525 – 2 November 1588) was a 16th-century [Bishop of Roermond](/wiki/Bishop_of_Roermond "Bishop of Roermond") and [Bishop of Ghent](/wiki/Bishop_of_Ghent "Bishop of Ghent").

## Biography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=William_Damasus_Lindanus&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Biography")]

William Damasus Lindanus was born at [Dordrecht](/wiki/Dordrecht "Dordrecht") in 1525, the son of Damasus van der Lint. He studied philosophy and theology at the [University of Leuven](/wiki/Old_University_of_Leuven "Old University of Leuven"), and having during this time applied himself also to [Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek "Ancient Greek") and [Hebrew](/wiki/Hebrew_language "Hebrew language"), went to [Paris](/wiki/Paris "Paris") to perfect himself in these languages. In 1552 he won the licenciate at Leuven, and the same year was ordained to the priesthood. Two years later, he was appointed professor of Sacred Scripture at the [University of Dillingen](/wiki/University_of_Dillingen "University of Dillingen").

In 1556, he took the [doctor's degree](/wiki/Doctorate "Doctorate") at Leuven, and was appointed [vicar-general](/wiki/Vicar-general "Vicar-general") to the [Bishop of Utrecht](/wiki/Archdiocese_of_Utrecht_(695%E2%80%931580) "Archdiocese of Utrecht (695–1580)") and dean of the chapter at the court chapel of the [Binnenhof](/wiki/Binnenhof "Binnenhof") in [The Hague](/wiki/The_Hague "The Hague"). Soon afterwards he became a royal counsellor and inquisitor in [Friesland](/wiki/Friesland "Friesland"). In 1562, the Habsburg king [Philip II of Spain](/wiki/Philip_II_of_Spain "Philip II of Spain") designated Lindanus for the newly erected [See of Roermond](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Diocese_of_Roermond "Roman Catholic Diocese of Roermond"), and the following year, on 4 April, he was consecrated in [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels") by [Cardinal Granvelle](/wiki/Cardinal_Granvelle "Cardinal Granvelle"). He was, however, unable to enter his diocese until 11 May 1569. The erection of this bishopric had caused displeasure throughout the [Low Countries](/wiki/Low_Countries "Low Countries"), especially in the country of [Guelders](/wiki/Guelders "Guelders"), of which Roermond was a part: where every act of the royal authority excited defiance. The Protestants were dissatisfied with the appointment of Lindanus, who was a staunch defender of the Catholic faith. The new bishop began at once to reform his diocese, assisted in person at the Provincial Synods of [Mechlin](/wiki/Mechlin "Mechlin") (1570) and of [Leuven](/wiki/Leuven "Leuven") (1573) and carried out the [Counter-Reformation](/wiki/Counter-Reformation "Counter-Reformation") laws and regulations of the [Council of Trent](/wiki/Council_of_Trent "Council of Trent").

In 1572, he was obliged to flee for several months from Roermond to the south of the Low Countries; on his return to his see, he defended vigorously the properties of the Church against the civil authorities. In 1573, a violent conflict broke out between himself and the [Duke of Alba](/wiki/Duke_of_Alba "Duke of Alba"); and the Protestants obliged him to flee on several occasions. In 1578, he travelled to [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome") and [Madrid](/wiki/Madrid "Madrid") in order to obtain justice against the [chapter](/wiki/Chapter_(religion) "Chapter (religion)") of [Maastricht](/wiki/Maastricht "Maastricht"), which had refused to execute the regulations concerning the episcopal endowment, and to confer with the Pope and the king upon the measures necessary for the safeguarding of the Faith in the Low Countries. Returning to Roermond, with the help of Philip II, he founded the royal seminary or college at Leuven, for the education of young clerics. Lindanus went to Rome again in 1584 to treat of the interests of his diocese and of the state of the Church in the Low Countries and in Germany; he insisted particularly upon the urgent necessity of replying in a scientific way to the [Centuriators of Magdeburg](/wiki/Centuriators_of_Magdeburg "Centuriators of Magdeburg"). His work in Roermond was brought to a close by his elevation to the See of Ghent, where he began his new episcopal duties on 22 July 1588. He died there three months later on 2 November.

## Writings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=William_Damasus_Lindanus&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Writings")]

Among his numerous works the following are especially worthy of mention: *De optimo scripturas interpretandi genere* (Cologne, 1558); *panoplia evangelica* (Cologne, 1560); *Stromatum libri III pro defensione Concilii Tridentini* (Cologne, 1575); *Missa apostolica* (Antwerp, 1589), and in a more popular form, the dialogues, *Dubitantius* and *Ruwardius* (Cologne, 1562-3).

He edited the academic discourses of [Ruard Tapperus](/wiki/Ruard_Tapper "Ruard Tapper") (1577–78), and wrote many works in Dutch for the instruction of his flock, in order to keep them from Protestantism and to refute the Confession of Antwerp of 1566.
